title: Humidity Monitoring and Dew Point Tracking (Meraki MT)
dataSources: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric IN ("humidity","temperature")` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Key fields: `serial`, `network.name`, `metric`, `humidity.relativePercentage`, `temperature.celsius`, `ts`.
spl:
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory" (metric="humidity" OR metric="temperature")
| rename "humidity.relativePercentage" as humidity_pct, "temperature.celsius" as temp_c
| stats latest(humidity_pct) as humidity_pct, latest(temp_c) as temp_c by serial, "network.name"
| where isnotnull(humidity_pct) AND isnotnull(temp_c)
| eval a=17.625, b=243.04
| eval alpha=ln(humidity_pct/100) + (a*temp_c)/(b+temp_c)
| eval dew_point_c=round((b*alpha)/(a-alpha), 1)
| eval comfort=case(humidity_pct < 30, "Too dry", humidity_pct > 60, "Too humid", true(), "Comfortable")
description: Monitors humidity levels to ensure optimal conditions for equipment and prevent moisture damage.
value: Monitors humidity levels to ensure optimal conditions for equipment and prevent moisture damage.
implementation: Configure `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` to collect both temperature and humidity readings from MT sensors. The Magnus-Tetens approximation derives dew point from temperature (¬∞C) and relative humidity (%). Alert when dew point approaches the surface temperature (condensation risk).
visualization: Humidity gauge per location; humidity vs temperature correlation; trend chart.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable (no CIM or Operational_Telemetry data model in this SPL).
knownFalsePositives: Temperature alarms during scheduled HVAC maintenance, controlled setback overrides, or sensor calibration.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric IN ("humidity","temperature")` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Key fields: `serial`, `network.name`, `metric`, `humidity.relativePercentage`, `temperature.celsius`, `ts`..
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Configure `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` to collect both temperature and humidity readings from MT sensors. The Magnus-Tetens approximation derives dew point from temperature (¬∞C) and relative humidity (%). Alert when dew point approaches the surface temperature (condensation risk).

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory" (metric="humidity" OR metric="temperature")
| rename "humidity.relativePercentage" as humidity_pct, "temperature.celsius" as temp_c
| stats latest(humidity_pct) as humidity_pct, latest(temp_c) as temp_c by serial, "network.name"
| where isnotnull(humidity_pct) AND isnotnull(temp_c)
| eval a=17.625, b=243.04
| eval alpha=ln(humidity_pct/100) + (a*temp_c)/(b+temp_c)
| eval dew_point_c=round((b*alpha)/(a-alpha), 1)
| eval comfort=case(humidity_pct < 30, "Too dry", humidity_pct > 60, "Too humid", true(), "Comfortable")
```

Understanding this SPL

**Humidity Monitoring and Dew Point Tracking (Meraki MT)** ‚Äî Monitors humidity levels to ensure optimal conditions for equipment and prevent moisture damage.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric IN ("humidity","temperature")` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Key fields: `serial`, `network.name`, `metric`, `humidity.relativePercentage`, `temperature.celsius`, `ts`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: meraki; **sourcetype**: meraki:sensorreadingshistory. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=meraki, sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by serial, "network.name"** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(humidity_pct) AND isnotnull(temp_c)` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **a** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **alpha** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **dew_point_c** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **comfort** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Humidity gauge per location; humidity vs temperature correlation; trend chart.
grandmaExplanation: We watch how your building systems behave. We help you know if something is off with this area: humidity monitoring and dew point tracking before harm, damage, or wasted effort piles up.
